## [00:00 - 03:03]

Okay, investment criteria. As I mentioned earlier, focus on 16 unit buildings, but ideally plan to focus on opportunities closer to 32. Targeting single family properties with too few units can be a major leverage issue for you going forward and they should be avoided entirely. I'm not a proponent of you dipping your feet in the water to test out the market to take baby steps will cost you money and it will cost you certainty. Think about it. The more scale you have, the more stability you have, more doors and more units mean more consistent income and that's the goal. That four unit building in the next economic downturn will get crushed. The only exception to this rule is if you're going to wholesale or fix and flip a smaller building, that's fine. But remember, that's not real estate investing. That's flipping. That's selling. If you're planning to really dive into real estate investing, avoid these little guy mentality at all costs. Small deals just are not worth the time at resources and energy. Also, this is where the foreclosures always take place because it's not ideal. Understanding risk versus stability. That's one criteria for future investment and it's important. Risk versus stability should always be front and center for you when you're considering a deal. If you're going to invest, you need to invest in things you know. It's about knowing your market, diving into that market versus worrying, crossing your fingers, throwing the dice, gambling. This is investing. And remember Warren Buffett's first rule, don't lose money. Second rule, don't lose money. I look at my own business and I see the positive impacts risk versus stability has had. If you have a lot of units, you'll inherently have more stability. I have nearly 5,000 units today. $700 million worth of real estate. I don't have up and down days. I'm stable because I have diversification because I have the scale of the units. Now, what to look for in an investment? What should you look for in a property? First and foremost, do you have scale? That kind of scale that breeds stability. 16 units? Okay, 32 units, better. That's why I created Cardone Capital because I know so many people, friends, family members, people that do business with me and my other companies that can't participate in scale. They don't know the market. They don't have time to shop the market. They can't find deals. They don't know who has these properties in the market. They can't afford the debt. So we go out and acquire these properties through Cardone Capital and let my friends, family, and customers invest alongside me. We see how important it is to scale and we want our friends and family members in. Now they can be. The second point here is look for income

## [03:03 - 05:46]

diversification. This naturally comes from scale. If you think about it, if you only have 32 units, you have 32 income sources. If you have 64, 600, 6000, then you have that many more income sources. That brings me to point 3, the middle of the road income. If the economy gets hammered and I'm always preparing for the hammering so that I can get through it, people will stop paying or they'll be able to pay less or they'll pay late. You still have that middle of the road income which in tougher economic times can be enough to get you through. Also, affordable housing. There's a shortage of affordable housing in this country. Rents that are 700 to even $1,400 a month depending upon your market is affordable income. 700 to $1,500 a month rent depending on the market you're in is still affordable income and affordable housing for many. I came out of the 2008 recession just fine. Why? Because my buildings were affordable housing, 700 to $1,500 a month rents and they continue to cash flow positive while I continue to build the scale in the organization. When everybody else was losing properties, their $4,000 a month rents, they were moving into my properties at $1,200. By the way, that was doing the worst housing climate in the history of the United States. Another error I can always focus on is control. I want to control the neighborhood if possible. I do not want to have one building or one property in one market. My ideal situation is I would have four different properties in every market that we go to. I want to control more than just my property. I want to control neighborhoods. That means looking at everything from how saturated an immediate market is to what competitions across the street. What are they charging? What amenities do they offer? How weak are they as managers? How much debt or little debt do they have? Can they withstand a pullback? I want to anticipate the area growing and or not growing and the rental needs going forward. In the short term and the long term, I have to know where the market's going and so do you. It all relates back to stability versus risk. Admittedly, I like risk in my deals because I have time and time and scale reduce my risk, particularly if I'm making good picks on locations. It makes sense. I want meaningful upside in everything I do, but I need to be paid while I wait. So I need to strike a balance and you will too between stability and risk.
